name: Iceland
hook: Icelanders don't have family names. A man named Jón has a son, Einar, who becomes Einar Jónsson — Jón's son — and a daughter who becomes Eva Jónsdóttir. A family of four routinely carries four different last names, nobody inherits one down the generations, and the phone book is alphabetized by first name. It's a small, young, volcanic country of under 400,000 people that kept a naming system the rest of Europe dropped centuries ago, along with a good deal else.

name: Italy
hook: Italy is the only country on Earth that completely surrounds two others. There are just three nations that sit entirely inside another country, and two of them are here — Vatican City, walled into a corner of Rome, and San Marino, perched on a mountain near Rimini. San Marino calls itself the oldest republic still standing: by its own telling, a stonecutter named Marinus founded it in the year 301, hiding from Rome, and it never stopped being independent.

name: Japan
hook: The world's oldest company was a Japanese temple-builder in business since the year 578. Kongō Gumi was founded to raise a Buddhist temple in Osaka, stayed in one family for over 1,400 years and forty generations building shrines and castles, and only lost its independence in 2006. Japan runs long like that. It holds more centuries-old businesses than any other country on Earth, including the oldest hotel still taking guests, open since the year 705.

name: United States
hook: The westernmost, northernmost, and easternmost state in the Union are all the same state. Alaska's Aleutian Islands run so far west they cross the 180th meridian into the Eastern Hemisphere, so the country wraps past the date line and comes out the other side. The rest of the map hides surprises like that at ground level — California is charted first, at full depth.

name: The East & Highlands
hook: Iceland's only wild reindeer live out here, in the east, descended from a handful brought over in the 1700s and never tamed. Beyond the eastern fjords rise the Highlands, the empty interior of the island: a volcanic desert of ash and glaciers with no towns, crossed by rough tracks that open only in high summer and close again with the first snow. It is one of the largest genuinely uninhabited spaces in Europe, and for much of the year nobody is in it at all.

name: The North
hook: The land in the north looks so little like Earth that NASA sent astronauts here to rehearse for the Moon. In the 1960s, Apollo crews trained in the black volcanic desert around Askja, learning to read geology on terrain that stood in for the lunar surface. The same region holds Dettifoss, the second most powerful waterfall in Europe after the Rhine Falls, a wall of grey glacial water hammering into a canyon, along with the boiling mud and pseudo-craters around Lake Mývatn.

name: Reykjavík & the Southwest
hook: The peninsula next to the capital woke up. After roughly eight hundred years of silence, Reykjanes — where the airport, the Blue Lagoon, and the town of Grindavík sit — began erupting in 2021 and has barely stopped since, a dozen eruptions in, fissures opening and lava swallowing roads a short drive from Reykjavík. This is where most Icelanders live and where nearly every visitor lands, on ground that has just turned young and dangerous again.

name: The South
hook: One of the world's oldest parliaments was founded in a crack between two continents. At Þingvellir, on the plain east of Reykjavík, Icelanders gathered to hold the Alþing from the year 930, and the plain itself is a rift valley where the North American and Eurasian plates pull apart. In the Silfra fissure nearby you can snorkel through glacial meltwater with a hand on each continent. The rest of the south is the classic Iceland: waterfalls, black-sand coast, glaciers, and the volcanoes underneath them.

name: The West & Westfjords
hook: When the first settlers arrived, the only land mammal already living in Iceland was the Arctic fox. Everything else on four legs — the horses, the sheep, the reindeer — came by boat with people. The fox got here on its own, probably across the sea ice, and it still owns the wildest corner of the country. The Westfjords are that corner: a shattered coastline of remote fjords and towering sea-bird cliffs where far more puffins live than people.

name: The Deep South
hook: People have lived in the caves of Matera for nine thousand years without a break — after Jericho and Aleppo, it may be the oldest continuously inhabited place on Earth. By the 1950s those same caves were so poor and malarial that the government called Matera "the shame of Italy" and evacuated all fifteen thousand residents. Then it came back as a UNESCO site and a film set. The deep south runs on that whiplash: Greek temples, whitewashed towns down in the heel and toe, and a history that swings from ancient wonder to recent hardship and back.

name: Emilia-Romagna
hook: There is a bank vault in Emilia-Romagna holding roughly four hundred thousand wheels of parmesan. Credem, an actual bank, takes young Parmigiano-Reggiano as loan collateral and ages it in climate-controlled warehouses until it's worth enough to sell — a loan you could, in the last resort, eat. It fits the place: this strip between the Po and the Apennines makes the parmesan, the prosciutto di Parma, the balsamic from Modena, and the tortellini everyone argues about, and treats all of it with the seriousness other regions reserve for gold.

name: Naples & Campania
hook: Everyone watches Vesuvius. The real monster is the other one. West of Naples, under the streets of Pozzuoli, sits Campi Flegrei — a supervolcano, Europe's largest active caldera, with roughly half a million people living inside it. The ground here breathes: it has been rising for years, lifting old harbors out of the sea and rattling the towns, a slow heave that people have lived with since Roman times. Naples built itself on top of the thing most likely to end it, and shrugged.

name: The North
hook: The pale towers of the Dolomites were once a coral reef. Two hundred and fifty million years ago the North was a warm, shallow tropical sea, and the atolls that built up on its floor hardened into rock before Africa slammed into Europe and shoved them three kilometers into the sky. The same region drops down to Milan's money and Venice's canals, but the mountains above are fossilized seabed, still full of the corals if you know where to look.

name: Rome & Lazio
hook: The spot where Julius Caesar was stabbed is now a cat sanctuary. Largo di Torre Argentina, a square of sunken Republican temples in the middle of Rome, holds the ruins of the hall where the Senate met and Caesar fell on the Ides of March, 44 BC — and for years its only tenants were stray cats, still run there as a no-kill shelter among the columns. Rome does this everywhere: the enormous past just sits in the road, half-forgotten, with the living city carrying on over and around it.

name: Sardinia
hook: The term "blue zone" was coined here. In the late 1990s, researchers hunting the world's longest-lived people drew a blue pen around the mountains of central Sardinia — Ogliastra and Barbagia — where men reach a hundred at rates found nowhere else, the only place on Earth where about as many men as women become centenarians. The island keeps its own counsel: thousands of Bronze Age stone towers, a language often called the closest thing left to spoken Latin, and shepherds still walking the distances their great-grandfathers walked.

name: Sicily
hook: Sicily lives next to a volcano that basically never stops. Mount Etna is the most active in Europe and one of the tallest, erupting so routinely that Catania's airport closing for ash is treated as weather, the mountain growing and shrinking with its own eruptions. People farm its slopes anyway, because the volcanic soil is that good. The whole island works this way, layered by everyone who ever took it — Greek temples kept better than Greece's, Arab-Norman churches, and a food and dialect that still remember all of them.

name: Tuscany & Umbria
hook: Before Rome, this was Etruscan country — a civilization that left about ten thousand inscriptions in an alphabet we can read and a language we still can't. You can sound out the words on their tombs and bronze mirrors; we mostly don't know what they mean. The Etruscans ran central Italy for centuries and handed Rome much of what became "Roman," then dissolved into it, leaving the cypress hills, the hilltop towns, and a written silence nobody has cracked.

name: Chubu (the Japan Alps)
hook: In spring you can walk a road with walls of snow twenty meters high on either side. When the Tateyama mountain route reopens each April, plows carve a corridor through the winter's buildup and leave the snow standing five or six stories tall on both sides of the lane. Chubu is Japan's mountainous spine, the Japan Alps and Mount Fuji, where the country stands up on end and the snow that buries it is measured against the same peaks that made it famous.

name: Chugoku
hook: Once a year, every god in Japan leaves home. In the old calendar October is Kannazuki, the month without gods, everywhere in the country but one place: the shrine of Izumo, on the Sea of Japan coast, where that same month is instead called the month with gods. All eight million deities of Shinto are said to travel there to sit in council over the coming year's marriages and fates. Chugoku is the quieter western end of the main island, holding both that myth-soaked coast and, on its other shore, Hiroshima.

name: Hokkaido
hook: Japan's north was barely Japan until modern times. Hokkaido is the homeland of the Ainu, a people with their own gods, their own tattoos, and a language related to nothing else on Earth, who were here long before settlers from the south colonized the island in the 1800s. Japan only formally recognized the Ainu as its indigenous people in 2019. The island still reads as a frontier: volcanoes, drift ice on the northern sea, some of the heaviest snowfall anywhere, and open space, which the rest of Japan does not have.

name: Kansai (Kyoto, Osaka, Nara)
hook: In Nara, the deer bow to you. Around thirteen hundred of them roam the old capital's parks and streets, protected for over a thousand years as messengers of the gods, and they have learned to dip their heads at visitors to be handed a cracker. It is equal parts sacred and shameless. Kansai is Japan's historical heart: Kyoto's thousand temples, Osaka's food, Nara's deer and its great bronze Buddha, the country's memory of itself from before Tokyo existed.

name: Kanto (Greater Tokyo)
hook: Greater Tokyo is the largest metropolitan area on Earth. Around thirty-seven million people live in the sprawl around the bay, moved each day by the busiest train network in the world. Kanto is the plain that holds it all, the capital and the crowds and the neon and the bullet trains fanning out from it. It is the Japan everyone pictures, and standing in the middle of it is still hard to take in.

name: Kyushu & Okinawa
hook: Fifty thousand people live inside a volcano. Mount Aso, in central Kyushu, has one of the largest calderas on Earth, a collapsed crater twenty-five kilometers across, and its floor is not empty: it holds towns, rice paddies, cattle, hot springs, and railway lines, all ringed by the old volcanic wall with an active cone still smoking in the middle. Kyushu is Japan's hot, volcanic south, steaming with onsen, and beyond it the Okinawan islands trail off toward the tropics with a language, a cuisine, and a former kingdom of their own.

name: Shikoku
hook: The smallest of Japan's main islands is wrapped by a pilgrimage. Twelve hundred kilometers of trail link eighty-eight temples in a loop all the way around Shikoku, walked for over a thousand years in the footsteps of the monk Kūkai. Pilgrims still make it, dressed in white, and the white is not only for purity: it was once a burial shroud, worn because you might die on the road and ought to be ready. Between the temples the island is deep valleys, swaying vine bridges, and some of the last genuinely rural Japan.

name: Tohoku
hook: There is a mountain in the north called Osorezan, which means Fear Mountain, and people go there to speak with the dead. It sits in a volcanic crater of bleached rock and steaming, sulfur-yellow pools, and the little stream running through it was long ago named for the river the dead must cross into the afterlife. At the July festival, blind mediums called itako fall into a trance and speak in the voices of the mourners' lost. Tohoku is Japan's deep, snowbound northeast, and it keeps the country's oldest and strangest beliefs closest to the surface.

name: California
hook: If California walked out of the Union tomorrow, it would leave carrying the world's fourth-largest economy — it passed Japan in 2024. It also carries sand dunes that sing, the rarest pine on Earth, and a lake two and a half times saltier than the sea, and almost none of that makes the guidebooks. This is the atlas's first branch, charted at full depth.

name: The Bay Area
hook: The Bay itself is partly a mining artifact. Gold Rush water cannons blasted a billion and a half cubic yards of the Sierra into the rivers, and the mud rode 150 miles down to build new shoreline, shallow the water, and lace the floor with mercury it still carries. The cities grew on top of that made-land, and the densest stretch of this atlas grew on top of the cities.

name: The Central Coast
hook: Just off Moss Landing, the sea floor drops into a canyon on the scale of the Grand Canyon — walls a mile high, running ninety-five miles out under the Pacific. That trench is why the whales come so close to shore here, and why marine-science institutes cluster on this one bend of coast. On land the seam runs the other direction: Big Sur's cliffs, otter-filled sloughs, and towns that never got around to modernizing.

name: The California Desert
hook: California's largest lake is an accident. In 1905 the Colorado River broke through a canal cut and poured into the desert for two years before anyone could stop it, and the Salton Sea has been slowly evaporating ever since. The desert seam holds the state's strangest register: dunes that sing when they slide, a mountain painted by one man over decades, and whole art towns built from what everyone else threw away.

name: The Sierra & Gold Country
hook: The largest living thing on Earth is a tree in the southern Sierra — General Sherman, more wood in one trunk than any other single organism — and the range thinks in that scale everywhere: granite walls half a mile tall, record snows, a ghost town frozen mid-abandonment on its eastern slope, and the foothill towns where the Gold Rush actually happened.

name: Southern California
hook: Los Angeles pumps oil from under its streets and pulls Ice Age bones from a tar seep beside a shopping district — the richest Pleistocene fossil site on Earth, still bubbling. Southern California runs on that kind of coexistence: surf town, desert edge, and twelve-lane metropolis sharing the same afternoon, with the strange parts hiding in plain sight.
